'''Tony Clubb''' (born 12 June 1987) is an English professional [[rugby league]] coach who is the assistant coach at the [[Leigh Leopards]] in the [[Betfred Super League]].

He is a former professional [[rugby league]] footballer who played as a {{rlp|pr}} for the [[Wigan Warriors]] in the [[Super League]] and has played for [[England national rugby league team|England]] at international level.<ref name="Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org"/> He played for London under the guises of [[Harlequins RL]] and the [[London Broncos]] in the [[Super League]].

==Background==
Clubb was born in [[Gravesend]], [[Kent]], England.

==London Rugby League==
He is a London Broncos club-trained player having featured in the first team from 2006 until 2013.

Tony Clubb started his rugby career playing for his local team Gravesend at Rugby Union. He switched to Rugby League after London Broncos helped found the [[Greenwich Admirals]] in 2001.  The Admirals produced several professional players including [[Louie McCarthy-Scarsbrook]], [[Dave Williams (rugby league, born 1989)|Dave Williams]] and [[Michael Worrincy]], each of who played for Greenwich, the London Broncos Academy and went on to play in the first team.{{citation needed|date=April 2024}}

By the time Clubb was ready to make his first team début, the London Broncos had been pushed into a kind of merger with Harlequins Rugby Union club and thus the team was called Harlequins RL by the time he played in the away fixture against the [[Bradford Bulls]] on 18 February 2006; a game which ended in a draw after the Bulls were awarded a controversial late equalizing penalty opportunity.

Clubb was seen as very much a fill in for the first team and was thus made available for the [[Harlequin F.C.|Harlequins]] rugby union side in the [[Middlesex Sevens]] at [[Twickenham Stadium]].<ref name="Amesbury to captain Quins Sevens squad">{{cite web|url=http://www.quins.co.uk/newspage.ink?nid=37715&newstype=p&matchid=&Refid=&storytype=RU|title=Amesbury to captain Quins Sevens squad|date=2007-08-19|publisher=Quins|access-date=2007-08-19}}</ref> He was, however, unable to play as he was called back for [[London Broncos|Harlequins RL]] to replace the unavailable [[Paul Sykes (rugby league)|Paul Sykes]] - who missed most of 2006's [[Super League XI]] - in the match against [[Wigan Warriors]].

Clubb was regarded as an outside back in London and featured mainly as a {{rlp|ce}} with the odd game as a {{rlp|wg}}. In 2008, England Rugby League was short of quality in the outside backs with the likes of Paul Sykes, [[Ade Gardner]], [[Mark Calderwood]] and [[Martin Gleeson (rugby league)|Martin Gleeson]] selected for the World Cup.
[[File:Tony Clubb.jpg|thumb|right|220px|Clubb playing for [[London Broncos#2006–2011: Harlequins Rugby League|Harlequins RL]]]]
Clubb thus represented England in the backs between 2008 and 2010.
[[File:Tony Clubb Quins.JPG|thumb|right|220px|Clubb playing for [[London Broncos#2006–2011: Harlequins Rugby League|Harlequins RL]]]]
After this, he became arguably a better player as he grew stronger and heavier and he started to play as an interchange forward but with England having much better forwards than backs, his international career was unintentionally ended by this switch.

He went on to play more than 150 games for Harlequins RL and London Broncos in all competitions, eventually becoming the Captain, though for a short period as he was agitating to leave the club as his enthusiasm waned.

In 2013, the team now known as London Broncos had serious financial problems and faced potential liquidation as owner David Hughes wobbled in his support.

The [[Wigan Warriors]]<ref name="Wigan Sign Sarginson and Clubb">{{cite web|url=http://www.wiganwarriors.com/WContent.aspx?id=8765&type=news|title=Wigan Sign Sarginson and Clubb|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150101165130/http://www.wiganwarriors.com/WContent.aspx?id=8765&type=news|archive-date=1 January 2015|url-status=dead|access-date=12 July 2017}}</ref> took full advantage by exploiting the situation to their fullest advantage. They approached Clubb despite him still having a contract, plus signed the out-of-contract England Knights half-back Dan Sarginson. London Broncos received a nominal fee for two of the best three club trained players they had ever produced and the next season was their last in Super League.<ref name="Wigan Sign Sarginson and Clubb" />

==Wigan Warriors==
=== 2014 ===
Clubb was a utility player perceived by some Wigan supporters as an outside back due to his international appearances but by the manager as a squad man who could play as a forward. He was on the bench 14 times, made 9 starts and was omitted from the squad for the other games.{{citation needed|date=April 2024}}

By the end of the season, he was mainly used as an interchange prop.

He also scored seven tries including a brace against his former side London Broncos.

He helped Wigan to the Super League Grand Final but was a losing finalist as Wigan lost against St Helens in a game infamous for Ben Flower knocking Lance Hohaia unconscious then continuing to attack him leading to a red card in the 3rd minute of the match.

Ironically his long-term friend Louis McCarthy-Scarsbrook was on the winning side.

=== 2015 ===
Clubb was moved permanently to the front row at the start of the new Super League season after expressing his desire to play at prop.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.wigantoday.net/sport/rugby-league/wigan-warriors/tony-aims-to-keep-place-in-the-clubb-1-7045310|title=Tony aims to keep place in the Clubb|website=www.wigantoday.net|language=en|access-date=2018-01-19}}</ref> Clubb made 26 appearances scoring tries against Wakefield Trinity and Warrington Wolves. In late May he signed a new deal just 18 months into his original 4-year contract due to his impressive performances and also the fact that his original deal did not really reflect his ability.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.wigantoday.net/sport/rugby-league/wigan-warriors/clubb-signs-new-deal-at-warriors-1-7281316|title=Clubb signs new deal at Warriors|website=www.wigantoday.net|language=en|access-date=2018-01-19}}</ref>

Clubb made his second appearance at Old Trafford in the Super League Grand Final and again did not win the Grand Final, losing out to the [[Leeds Rhinos]] 22–20 at [[Old Trafford]].<ref name="Leeds pip Wigan to seal treble after brilliant, breathless Grand Final">{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2015/oct/10/leeds-wigan-super-league-grand-final-match-report|title=Leeds pip Wigan to seal treble after brilliant, breathless Grand Final|publisher=Guardian|date=10 October 2015|access-date=8 November 2019}}</ref>

=== 2016 ===
Clubb started the season impressively making 15 appearances and scoring one try before an illegal crusher tackle against Hull FC saw him requiring surgery. Due to the crusher tackle he did not play for another nine months meaning he was not part of the squad that won the 2016 Super League Grand Final against Warrington, thus he had still not been on the winning side at Old Trafford despite Wigan's three visits in three years.{{citation needed|date=August 2023}}

=== 2017 ===
After nine months out he made his comeback against Salford Red Devils in Round 1 of Super League. He played an important role against Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks helping Wigan to win the World Club Challenge for a record-breaking fourth time. His early season form was not overly impressive and after he withdrew early against Castleford Tigers it was announced that he was to have surgery to remove a kidney which had been troubling him. Incredibly just two months later he made his comeback against Widnes Vikings helping Wigan to a 28–12 victory.

He played an important role in the narrow Challenge Cup final loss, his controversially disallowed try the reason Hull FC claimed the trophy. Hull coach Lee Radford said after the game "I honestly thought that was a try for Tony Clubb".<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.seriousaboutrl.com/tony-clubb-try-9517|title=Radford: I thought Tony Clubb's was a try - Serious About Rugby League|date=2017-08-27|work=Serious About Rugby League|access-date=2018-01-19|language=en-GB}}</ref> After the ball was stolen from Clubb he grounded the ball however the video referee deemed he had lost control of the ball, therefore, had knocked on.<ref name="Hull FC 18-14 Wigan recap as the Black and Whites claim back-to-back Challenge Cup triumphs">{{cite web|url=https://www.mirror.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/challenge-cup-final-live-score-11054626|title=Hull FC 18-14 Wigan recap as the Black and Whites claim back-to-back Challenge Cup triumphs|publisher=Mirror|date=26 August 2017|access-date=6 November 2019}}</ref>

The controversy increased a week later when St Helens were awarded a similar try against Wigan in the Super League. In total Clubb played 22 times scoring one try for a feeble Wigan team that finished in the bottom half of the table.

=== 2018 ===
In the 2018 season, Wigan Warriors released eight players including stars like McIlorum and Gelling but made only two signings.  This saw many players handed a lower number than before and Clubb was handed the number 8 shirt after Wigan released Frank-Paul Nu'uausala. This was the first time since his days at London Broncos that Clubb was given a number that suggested he was in the starting line-up.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.totalrl.com/wigan-confirm-2018-squad-numbers-interesting-change|title=Wigan confirm 2018 squad numbers - with an interesting change. - Total Rugby League|date=2018-01-16|work=Total Rugby League|access-date=2018-01-19|language=en-US}}</ref>

He played in the [[2018 Super League Grand Final]] victory over the [[Warrington Wolves]] at [[Old Trafford]].<ref name="Super League Grand Final 2018: Wigan 12-4 Warrington – as it happened">{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/live/2018/oct/13/super-league-grand-final-2018-wigan-warriors-v-warrington-wolves-live?page=with:block-5bc1ff43e4b05798be94cc63#liveblog-navigation|title=Super League Grand Final 2018: Wigan 12-4 Warrington – as it happened|publisher=Guardian|date=13 October 2018|access-date=5 November 2019}}</ref>

===2019===
Clubb played 23 games for games for Wigan in 2019 including their shock semi-final loss against Salford.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/wigan-vs-salford/52328/|title=Salford seal maiden Super League Grand Final appearance|website=www.skysports.com}}</ref>

===2020===
Clubb played in the [[2020 Super League Grand Final]] which Wigan lost 8–4 against St Helens.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/55072997|title=Super League Grand Final: Wigan 4-8 St Helens|work=BBC Sport |date=25 November 2020 }}</ref>

===2021===
In round 5 of the [[2021 Super League season]], Clubb was placed on report for an alleged racist remark towards [[Hull FC]] player [[Andre Savelio]]. He was subsequently suspended from playing by Wigan until the investigation was concluded.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://wiganwarriors.com/blog/2021/04/30/club-statement-2/|title=wiganwarriors.com|date=30 April 2021 }}</ref>

On 4 May, Clubb was suspended for eight matches and handed a £500 fine for his on-field comments towards Savelio. It was alleged by Savelio that Clubb had called him a "stupid Polynesian c***". At the hearing, Clubb claimed he said to Savelio “You silly c***, you’re from Warrington. You’re not Polynesian you c***".<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/12204/12296991/tony-clubb-wigan-forward-handed-eight-match-ban-for-andre-savelio-comment/|title= Wigan forward handed eight-match ban for Andre Savelio comment|website=www.skysports.com}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.wigantoday.net/sport/rugby-league/revealed-what-tony-clubb-claims-he-really-said-to-andre-savelio-3227279/|title=What Tony Clubb claims he really said to Andre Savelio|website=www.wigantoday.net}}</ref>
Clubb announced he would retire at the end of the [[Super League XXVI|2021 super league]] season on 22 September 2021.<ref>{{Cite news|title=Wigan's Clubb retiring at end of season|language=en-GB|work=BBC Sport|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/58658080|access-date=2021-09-23}}</ref>

==International honours==
Clubb has previously been selected in the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup squads#England|England training squad]] for the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup]].<ref name="Myler gets England call">{{cite web|url=http://www.englandrl.co.uk/article.php?id=11890|title=Myler gets England call|publisher=England Rugby League|date=2008-09-29|access-date=2008-10-03|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081007232803/http://www.englandrl.co.uk/article.php?id=11890|archive-date=7 October 2008|df=dmy-all}}</ref>

He was selected for the team to face [[Wales national rugby league team|Wales]] at the [[Keepmoat Stadium]] prior to England's departure for the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup]].<ref name="Gleeson to lead new-look England">{{cite web|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/7642490.stm|title=Gleeson to lead new-look England|publisher=BBC|date=2008-10-09|access-date=2008-10-10}}</ref>

He made his [[England national rugby league team|England]] début in the victory over [[Wales national rugby league team|Wales]] on 10 October 2008.<ref name="England warm up in style">{{cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12196_4297504,00.html|title=England warm up in style|publisher=Sky Sports|date=2008-10-10|access-date=2008-10-11}}</ref>

On 6 November 2010 he became the tenth ever England rugby league player to score 4 tries in an international fixture. This was achieved in England's 36–10 win over Papua New Guinea in their last match of the [[2010 Four Nations]] tournament.{{citation needed|date=April 2024}}

==Honours==
Clubb has, as of 2021, played for Wigan for eight seasons, securing two major honours.

He won one League Leader's Shield in 2020 and the Grand Final in 2018.{{citation needed|date=August 2023}}

He has missed out on five more honours, losing in the Grand Final in 2014, 2015, and 2020, and the Challenge Cup Final in 2017 plus missing one successful final due to injury.
